Word count is a terrible metric for writers.  I've been working on my Bitcoin Novel for over two years now.  It's currently 71k words.  Or only 100 words/day.  Which makes me seem glacially slow at writing... but that doesn't tell the whole story.  Sometimes I chop off gigantic sections and that's the best thing for the story.  Other times I rewrite an entire chapter for the third time because it isn't quite working.  The longer I've been doing this the more I think the best metric is simply how much time you spent in deep focused working sessions.   Trying to jam creative output into a single metric so you get a dopamine hit can be counter intuitive to the real goal: create a great story.  Great fiction requires great sacrifice.  One of the deepest lessons I've learned from #Bitcoin is that anything worth making requires Proof of Work and Low Time Preference  "Once you know the way broadly, you will see it in all things."

Let's take @rajatsoni's math a bit further.  If you bought $100 of #Bitcoin at the very top of $69k in 2021, and kept buying $100 every single month, you'd have $7128 now.  If someone in Gen Z saved $100 in stocks, $100 dollars in bonds, or $100 towards a down payment they would never, ever, be able to save enough for retirement.  The current system gives them no hope.  But if they were to instead learn about #Bitcoin and use it as a savings account they would actually have hope for their financial future.  If they continue saving that $100 per month for the next 15 years, they'll have somewhere between $500k and $1MM by 2040.

Inflation disproportionately harms those who are the poorest in society.  If you've studied #Bitcoin you know this to be true.  Nothing illustrates this better than the game of Monopoly.   Imagine you're playing Monopoly with four people, Player 1 has taken complete control over everything in the board.  They've got hotels on Boardwalk all the way down to Mediterranean Ave.  If you are Players 2 - 4 almost anywhere you land is going to cost you some serious cash.  But all three players are out of money.  Player 1 now has all of the $20k that comes in the standard Monopoly game.  This isn't very fair... So the Bank decides to invent some extra money it gives $2,000 more dollars to every single one of these poor players.  Inflating the money supply by 30%  For a minute they're excited, the game keeps going.   Then player two lands in St James place.  Player 3 and Player 4 land on St. Charlies Place and Virginia Ave.   By the time they've went all the way around the board the rich player now has $26k.   Clearly we didn't give the poor people enough money!  This time the bank hands out $10,000 extra to each of the players, inflating the money supply by 115%  But nothing has changed.   Player one isn't selling his scarce assets.  They go around a few more times and now player number one has $56k dollars.   The longer this game continues the larger the gap gets between Player 1 and the other three.   It doesn't matter how much money the government (the bank) gives into the hands of the poor people.   It doesn't change the game.  It doesn't change the principles of economic scarcity.  This is the exact same game theory that plays out in our current Fiat financial system every time our government injects more money into the system.  It doesn't matter if they give it to the poor people, those with more assets will always win.  Fortunately #Bitcoin gives us all a way to escape this vicious cycle.

Question:   I've heard that Bitcoin is very slow. How could it ever possibly become globally used for money?   Answer:  This is a very popular critique often given by someone with a slight misunderstanding of the underlying problem Bitcoin is trying to solve.  Think about the US dollar for a minute (by a gigantic margin, the thing most people measure value in and transact with).  What is its main use case?  0.01% of all US dollars are actively being traded from one person to another in a financial transaction.  99.9% of all US dollars are simply being held.  Some of this as numbers on a bank website. Some of this as paper dollars buried in the backyard.  The vast majority of our time using dollars is not transacting, but to retain value between spending periods.  This is the thing that Bitcoin is optimizing for.  Value retention.  Instead of quick transaction speeds, or low volatility, Bitcoin optimizes for having the properties of perfect scarcity.  There will never be more than 21 million total Bitcoin.  So while the US might print more dollars in attempt to reduce the dollars "volatility" Bitcoin embraces this volatility and simply remains unchanged in the face of whatever else is happening on the planet.  Bitcoin tries to be the most stable measuring stick in this wildly unstable world.  A store of value that can never be debased.

@Oliverderci1 That's a very well articulated question!  Most of my price predictions are for the sake of having a little bit of fun, but if I had to give a serious one I think the absolute bottom if there were to be some extremely serious "black swan" kind of event would be the range we chopped around at all of last summer.  $58k to $70k.  IMO no matter what happens we never go below that again.  I very publicly (and incorrectly) thought we would never dip below $100k again before we did in June. FWIW I still think that's the case and that we are on the precipice of a pretty sizable more higher.  All the other questions are really dependent on how the rest of the bull market plays out.  For example, if we see something wild like $1MM Bitcoin I would expect something like an 80% drop down to like $200k.... but if we have this very choppy and orderly bull market with much less euphoria I would think that anything more than a 50% drop is unlikely and we have a super drawn out cycle.  I don't have anything more concrete than that though.  I think $140k is like 90% likely by the end of the year.  I think $200k is like 55% likely within the next year.   Note of caution though that I'm truly not some market expert, I'm just on here learning in public and am very frequently wrong.
